runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production in the U.S.
citations: Chirinko, R. S. (2008). Sigma: The long and short of it. Journal of Macroeconomics, 30(2), 671-686.
Karabarbounis, L., & Neiman, B. (2014). The global decline of the labor share. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 129(1), 61-103.
Oberfield, E., & Raval, D. (2021). Micro data and macro technology. Econometrica, 89(2), 703-732.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the macro elasticity of substitution between capital and labor typically find values less than 1 (Cobb-Douglas). Time-series and micro-aggregated estimates (e.g., Chirinko, Oberfield & Raval) often center around 0.4 to 0.7. However, some macro models explaining the declining labor share via falling capital prices (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) require an elasticity greater than 1. A consensus places the long-run elasticity strongly below 1, usually around 0.6 to 0.7.

    "Oberfield, E., & Raval, D. (2021). Micro data and macro technology. Econometrica, 89(2), 703-732."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the macro elasticity of substitution between capital and labor typically find values less than 1 (Cobb-Douglas). Time-series and micro-aggregated estimates (e.g., Chirinko, Oberfield & Raval) often center around 0.4 to 0.7. However, some macro models explaining the declining labor share via falling capital prices (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) require an elasticity greater than 1. A consensus places the long-run elasticity strongly below 1, usually around 0.6 to 0.7."
}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production in the U.S.
citations: Chirinko (2008) - Review of empirical estimates suggesting the elasticity is robustly less than 1.
Oberfield and Raval (2021) - Micro-data based estimate showing an aggregate elasticity of around 0.7.
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - Macro estimate suggesting an elasticity greater than 1 (approx 1.25) to explain the global decline in the labor share.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the elasticity of substitution between capital and labor vary widely. Microeconomic and firm-level studies typically find values well below 1, often between 0.4 and 0.7. Macroeconomic estimates are generally higher, sometimes reaching or exceeding 1, particularly in models that attempt to explain the declining labor share via cheaper investment goods. I center my estimate at 0.75, reflecting a strong weight on the recent structural micro-to-macro literature (e.g., Oberfield and Raval), with a right tail extending past 1 to account for alternative macro estimates.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production in the U.S.",
  "point_estimate": 0.75,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.30,
    "p25": 0.50,
    "p50": 0.75,
    "p75": 0.95,
    "p95": 1.25
  },
  "citations": [
    "Chirinko (2008) - Review of empirical estimates suggesting the elasticity is robustly less than 1.",
    "Oberfield and Raval (2021) - Micro-data based estimate showing an aggregate elasticity of around 0.7.",
    "Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - Macro estimate suggesting an elasticity greater than 1 (approx 1.25) to explain the global decline in the labor share."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the elasticity of substitution between capital and labor vary widely. Microeconomic and firm-level studies typically find values well below 1, often between 0.4 and 0.7. Macroeconomic estimates are generally higher, sometimes reaching or exceeding 1, particularly in models that attempt to explain the declining labor share via cheaper investment goods. I center my estimate at 0.75, reflecting a strong weight on the recent structural micro-to-macro literature (e.g., Oberfield and Raval), with a right tail extending past 1 to account for alternative macro estimates."
}

However, cross-sectional and micro-to-macro aggregation approaches heavily favor an aggregate elasticity in the 0.5 to 0.8 range.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Chirinko (2008), 'sigma: The long and short of it' - reviews empirical estimates and argues for a value around 0.4 to 0.6.
Oberfield and Raval (2014), 'Micro Data and Macro Technology' - uses plant-level data to estimate aggregate elasticity around 0.7.
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - argue for an elasticity greater than 1 (around 1.25) to explain the global decline in the labor share.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution are highly debated. Time-series macro estimates (e.g., Antras 2004, Chirinko 2008) frequently find values well below 1 (0.4-0.6), implying capital and labor are gross complements. Micro-aggregated approaches (like Oberfield and Raval) estimate values around 0.7. Conversely, some macroeconomists (e.g., Karabarbounis and Neiman, Piketty) argue for values greater than 1 to explain long-term trends like the declining labor share. My median reflects the strong empirical consensus that the value is strictly less than 1, while the right tail accounts for the prominent minority view.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.",
  "point_estimate": 0.65,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.35,
    "p25": 0.45,
    "p50": 0.65,
    "p75": 0.85,
    "p95": 1.25
  },
  "citations": [
    "Chirinko (2008), 'sigma: The long and short of it' - reviews empirical estimates and argues for a value around 0.4 to 0.6.",
    "Oberfield and Raval (2014), 'Micro Data and Macro Technology' - uses plant-level data to estimate aggregate elasticity around 0.7.",
    "Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - argue for an elasticity greater than 1 (around 1.25) to explain the global decline in the labor share."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution are highly debated. Time-series macro estimates (e.g., Antras 2004, Chirinko 2008) frequently find values well below 1 (0.4-0.6), implying capital and labor are gross complements. Micro-aggregated approaches (like Oberfield and Raval) estimate values around 0.7. Conversely, some macroeconomists (e.g., Karabarbounis and Neiman, Piketty) argue for values greater than 1 to explain long-term trends like the declining labor share. My median reflects the strong empirical consensus that the value is strictly less than 1, while the right tail accounts for the prominent minority view."
}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Chirinko (2008) - 'Sigma: The long and short of it' (surveys empirical estimates, often concluding sigma is significantly less than 1, around 0.4-0.6).
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - 'The Global Decline of the Labor Share' (estimated sigma > 1, around 1.25, to explain declining labor share).
Oberfield and Raval (2014) - 'Micro Data and Macro Technology' (estimate aggregate elasticity from plant-level data, finding it to be around 0.7).
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the macro elasticity of substitution between capital and labor generally fall below 1 (Cobb-Douglas). Time-series estimates often yield values between 0.4 and 0.6. Some recent macro literature (e.g., Karabarbounis and Neiman) argues for a value greater than 1 to explain the declining labor share, but micro-aggregated studies (like Oberfield and Raval) tend to find values around 0.6 to 0.8. I place the median at 0.65, reflecting the strong weight of evidence for capital-labor complementarity at the aggregate level, with a 95% upper bound slightly above 1 to account for the literature supporting substitution > 1.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.",
  "point_estimate": 0.65,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.40,
    "p25": 0.55,
    "p50": 0.65,
    "p75": 0.85,
    "p95": 1.10
  },
  "citations": [
    "Chirinko (2008) - 'Sigma: The long and short of it' (surveys empirical estimates, often concluding sigma is significantly less than 1, around 0.4-0.6).",
    "Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) - 'The Global Decline of the Labor Share' (estimated sigma > 1, around 1.25, to explain declining labor share).",
    "Oberfield and Raval (2014) - 'Micro Data and Macro Technology' (estimate aggregate elasticity from plant-level data, finding it to be around 0.7)."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the macro elasticity of substitution between capital and labor generally fall below 1 (Cobb-Douglas). Time-series estimates often yield values between 0.4 and 0.6. Some recent macro literature (e.g., Karabarbounis and Neiman) argues for a value greater than 1 to explain the declining labor share, but micro-aggregated studies (like Oberfield and Raval) tend to find values around 0.6 to 0.8. I place the median at 0.65, reflecting the strong weight of evidence for capital-labor complementarity at the aggregate level, with a 95% upper bound slightly above 1 to account for the literature supporting substitution > 1."
}

runs.reasoningSummary: Most empirical macroeconomic literature rejects the Cobb-Douglas assumption of an elasticity exactly equal to 1, typically finding values between 0.4 and 0.8 for the U.S. aggregate economy.
